## Cancer Patients' Knowledge, Attitudes, and Willingness Toward Long-Term Care

### Patient Understanding of Available Long-Term Care Options

A foundational challenge in cancer survivorship is that many patients finish active treatment with a limited understanding of what long-term care (LTC) entails, who is responsible for providing it, and what late effects they should anticipate. Evidence drawn from multiple survivor cohorts consistently identifies a knowledge deficit as the primary barrier to effective engagement with post-treatment services. In a modified Delphi study of young adult survivors of childhood cancer, Zebrack and colleagues found that the most prominent self-reported barrier to follow-up care was "lack of knowledge on the part of both physicians and survivors regarding long-term health issues related to cancer," with survivors articulating clear desires for information about their medical histories, psychosocial support resources, and the trajectory of potential late effects [@Zebrack2004]. This knowledge gap is not self-resolving over time: Martin and colleagues, describing the OPTILATER protocol enrolling up to 3,300 long-term cancer survivors across multiple disease types in Germany, documented that "existing healthcare systems are insufficiently adapted to meet their ongoing needs, particularly for long-term survivors who may experience physical, emotional, and socio-economic hardships" [@Martin2025]. The structural invisibility of LTC options thus compounds an already fragile patient awareness.

Among hematological cancer populations—where intensive treatments such as blood and marrow transplantation (BMT) and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) generate complex, life-long surveillance needs—patient understanding of care transitions is particularly limited. A qualitative study by McErlean and colleagues interviewing 22 BMT survivors and six carers in New South Wales found that participants held "diverse views on the availability of community BMT expertise," with rural and regional survivors especially uncertain about whether competent follow-up existed near their homes [@McErlean2026]. Similarly, Bell and Swainston's qualitative exploration of 17 HSCT recipients in England identified a dominant theme of uncertainty at the point of transfer to long-term follow-up (LTFU) clinics: survivors questioned "will there be a change in my care, or will appointments just become less frequent?" and reported that "there was not much information about what to expect or be aware of" in terms of late-effects screening [@Bell2024]. These accounts reveal that even patients who are actively engaged with specialist teams often cannot articulate the structure, purpose, or duration of the care they will receive.

Communication during follow-up consultations, which represents a principal channel through which patients acquire LTC knowledge, is frequently inadequate. Mellblom and colleagues video-recorded 76 consultations between pediatric oncologists and adolescent survivors of leukemia and lymphoma, finding that while late effects were discussed in 85% of appointments, oncologists provided only "basic" information in 30% and "extended" information in just 29% of relevant discussions—leaving 41% of cases with no substantive information transfer at all [@Mellblom2015]. This variability was not explained by patient age, time since treatment, or clinical risk of late effects, but was instead attributable primarily to individual oncologist practice style, underscoring the lack of standardized information protocols.

### Attitudes and Emotional Responses to Long-Term Care

Cancer survivors' emotional orientation toward LTC is multidimensional, shaped simultaneously by relief at completing treatment, anxiety about disease recurrence, and uncertainty regarding bodily symptoms that were previously managed within the oncology team. Heathcote and colleagues articulate this tension precisely, noting that cancer survival is "characterised by uncertainty, fear, and the interpretation of bodily sensations as potentially symptomatic of cancer recurrence," and that fear can lead to "over-vigilance about bodily sensations and precautionary visits to the doctor, both of which can increase the chance of early detection but can also increase anxiety and decrease quality of life" [@Heathcote2018]. This psychological burden means that many patients do not experience LTC as a routine care transition but as an emotionally charged renegotiation of their relationship with illness and identity.

Among BMT survivors, the relational dimension of LTC attitudes is paramount. McErlean and colleagues identified "relationships with health professionals" as one of two central themes in their qualitative analysis, with survivors placing high value on the perceived expertise and continuity of their treating teams [@McErlean2026]. While some participants expressed willingness to transition to community-based primary care, concerns about the technical capacity of generalists to manage BMT-specific complications were widespread—particularly in rural settings where specialist oversight was geographically inaccessible. Bell and Swainston similarly found that HSCT recipients in England drew significant psychological reassurance from "remaining on a healthcare pathway and maintaining relationships with healthcare professionals," with care coordination—knowing "it is good to know I am still in the system"—serving as a proxy indicator of safety and ongoing clinical attention [@Bell2024].

Attitudes toward LTC also reflect broader emotional responses to the cancer experience that were established during active treatment. Zebrack and colleagues noted that young adult survivors of childhood cancer express desires for psychosocial support, social advocacy, and medical history documentation that extend well beyond biomedical follow-up, reflecting an understanding of LTC that encompasses the whole person [@Zebrack2004]. Breast cancer patients navigating financial toxicity—a near-universal feature of long-term cancer care in the literature—demonstrate emotional responses ranging from pervasive negativity to deliberate positive reframing, with qualitative meta-synthesis by Jiahui and colleagues identifying "coping emotions" including both "shrouded in negative emotions" and "adopting a positive attitude" as distinct adaptive stances taken by the same population in response to the chronic financial demands of care [@Jiahui2025]. This affective complexity resists simple characterization and demands LTC models that address emotional experience as a clinical endpoint in its own right.

### Willingness to Accept Care and Factors Influencing Decisions

Patient willingness to engage with long-term follow-up services is not uniform and is shaped by multiple overlapping determinants, including disease type, care setting, trust in providers, perceived competence of the referring system, and the clarity of communication at transition points. Across hematological malignancies, willingness to transfer from specialist to primary care settings is present but conditional. In the McErlean study, some BMT survivors expressed openness to community-based models of care, but this willingness was contingent on adequate communication and coordination between transplant centres and local providers, clear delineation of professional roles, and access to telehealth for specialist consultation when needed [@McErlean2026]. Where these conditions were not met, patients resisted transition and expressed preferences for indefinite retention within specialist follow-up.

In pediatric and adolescent oncology, the question of willingness is complicated by developmental transitions. Eshelman-Kent and colleagues, surveying 179 Children's Oncology Group (COG) institutions, found that 47% of institutions reported models in which adult survivors transitioned to adult primary care providers for risk-based health care, while 44% retained survivors indefinitely at the treating cancer centre [@EshelmanKent2011]. The critical issue documented was the "perceived insufficient knowledge on the part of the clinician receiving the transition referral"—a concern that, when communicated to patients or families, suppressed willingness to accept transfer and reinforced preferences for specialist-centred care. For adolescent survivors of leukemia and lymphoma, unwillingness to engage with self-monitoring behaviors also reflects incomplete information provision: without reliable extended information about what late effects to expect and how to respond to them, Mellblom and colleagues note that survivors cannot engage meaningfully in self-management, reducing their autonomous participation in LTC [@Mellblom2015].

Willingness to accept care is further influenced by perceived access barriers and financial considerations. For breast cancer survivors, financial toxicity constitutes a structural constraint on willingness: even patients who express positive attitudes toward LTC engagement report "constrained decisions" driven by cost, with Jiahui and colleagues documenting that coping behaviors including "avoidance" and "financial planning" are common adaptive responses to the perceived unaffordability of sustained follow-up [@Jiahui2025]. Survivorship care plan (SCP) provision—a concrete mechanism for structuring patient willingness and engagement—remains strikingly rare. Blanch-Hartigan and colleagues, drawing on a nationally representative survey of 1,130 oncologists and 1,020 primary care physicians in the United States, found that while 64% of oncologists reported "always or almost always discussing survivorship care recommendations" with survivors, fewer than 5% provided a written SCP to the patient, and only 12% of primary care physicians reported survivorship discussions with survivors [@BlanchHartigan2014]. The absence of a written document with which patients can actively engage represents a missed opportunity to formalize and support their willingness to participate in their own long-term care.

### Cultural, Demographic, and Disease-Related Factors

The heterogeneity of patient knowledge, attitudes, and willingness toward LTC is structured by cancer type, disease stage, demographic characteristics, and cultural context. Among patients with relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) who are eligible for novel cellular therapies such as CAR-T, patient hesitancy and "poor understanding of CAR-T therapy" are documented as distinct barriers to accessing and accepting advanced care pathways, compounded by logistical barriers including required travel to academic treatment centers and limited caregiver support [@Hoffmann2023]. This profile—where disease-specific complexity intersects with individual knowledge deficits—illustrates how willingness to engage with high-intensity long-term treatment is not merely a function of patient preference but of the information environment in which decisions are made.

Demographic factors, including age, migration background, and geographic location, modulate engagement with LTC across studies. Martin and colleagues' OPTILATER protocol specifically incorporates "survivors from diverse socio-demographic backgrounds, including those with a migration history," as a target group of investigation, recognizing that "unique challenges" including language barriers, cultural health beliefs, and differential access to care intersect with disease-specific needs to produce distinct survivorship trajectories [@Martin2025]. Rural and regional residence consistently emerges as a negative modifier of both LTC access and the patient experience of care quality; McErlean and colleagues document that rural BMT survivors expressed particular anxiety about the availability of community BMT expertise and were more likely to prefer indefinite specialist retention as a compensatory strategy [@McErlean2026].

Cancer stage and treatment intensity shape patient expectations and attitudes toward LTC in predictable ways. Among patients who have received immunotherapy with immune checkpoint inhibitors, long-term care planning must extend to managing treatment-induced endocrine dysfunction, including hypophysitis, for which Di Stasi and colleagues document that "long-term care depends on the severity of hypophysitis, and the specific hormonal axes involved"—requiring individualized, multidisciplinary monitoring that patients may not have anticipated when consenting to immunotherapy [@Stasi2025]. Similarly, in fertility-preserved female cancer patients, Ehrbar and colleagues demonstrated that online decision aids improved knowledge and attitudes toward fertility options but that knowledge gains were not uniformly sustained over time, and that decisional regret was associated with incomplete information at the point of decision making [@V2021]. These findings underscore that disease-related complexity generates specific, often underestimated long-term care needs that patients must understand to accept and engage with appropriately.

### Unmet Information Needs

Across all cancer types, disease stages, and demographic groups, unmet information needs represent the most consistently documented gap in the patient experience of LTC. Zebrack and colleagues' Delphi analysis of young adult survivors identified self-advocacy training for survivors as the principal recommendation generated by patients themselves, reflecting an understanding that navigating LTC requires skills—assertiveness in seeking follow-up, familiarity with treatment history, and the ability to communicate risk—that are rarely explicitly taught [@Zebrack2004]. In HSCT populations, Bell and Swainston document that survivors entering LTFU clinics in England experience "uncertainty and lack of information regarding the transfer from acute to long-term care and clinic screening practices," and that this uncertainty is not resolved by clinical encounters characterized by brevity and insufficient explanation [@Bell2024].

Among cancer survivors with physical activity support needs, Dore and colleagues describe ongoing challenges in delivering physical activity programs both clinically and in community settings, noting that despite the established benefit of physical activity as a long-term care intervention, patients and healthcare providers alike identify "facilitators and barriers" that prevent uptake—a finding consistent with the broader literature suggesting that patients require structured information and motivational support to engage with self-management components of LTC [@Dor2022]. Breast cancer patients facing financial toxicity express specific unmet needs for "health insurance policy support, medical institutional support, non-medical institutional resources support, financial information needs, and financial management needs"—a multi-domain information deficit that extends well beyond clinical information and encompasses the socioeconomic scaffolding of sustained cancer care [@Jiahui2025].

The gap between what patients need to know and what they are told remains both wide and consequential. Blanch-Hartigan and colleagues demonstrate that PCPs who received SCPs from oncologists were 9 times more likely (95% CI, 5.74 to 14.82) to report survivorship discussions with survivors, establishing a causal chain in which inadequate information transfer at the provider level perpetuates inadequate information transfer at the patient level [@BlanchHartigan2014]. Addressing unmet information needs in cancer survivors thus requires systemic interventions—standardized communication protocols, written care plans, decision aids, and structured transitions—rather than reliance on individual clinical encounter quality, which the evidence consistently shows to be highly variable and systematically insufficient for the scale of the survivorship population's informational demands.
